romania.title: Se dă startul la proiectarea tronsoanelor 1 și 3 ale Autostrăzii Moţca - Iaşi – Ungheni. Când s-ar putea intra în faza de execuție
romania.summary: Compania Naţională de Investiţii Rutiere (CNIR) a emis Ordinul pentru începerea proiectării tronsoanelor 1 şi 3 ale Autostrăzii Moţca - Iaşi - Ungheni, debutul efectiv al etapei de proiectare urmând să aibă loc pe data de 24 august. Se dă astfel startul pentru încă 45 de kilometri ai Autostrăzii Unirii A8, a anunţat, vineri, secretarul de stat în Ministerul Transporturilor şi Infrastructurii (MTI), Horaţiu Cosma.

Absolventă a Drexel University din Philadelphia și cu experiență profesională acumulată atât în SUA, cât și în România, ea s-a specializat în dezvoltarea brandurilor din zona de wellness și personal development. medical.title: A higher-dose flu shot could spare millions of older adults a hospital stay
medical.summary: Influenza is a seasonal condition that causes coughing, sneezing, mild fever and aches in most cases. However, it can sometimes take a serious turn, leading to hospitalization, especially for young children, adults over 65 and pregnant people. A recent study published in JAMA Network Open examined whether the high-dose inactivated influenza vaccine (HD-IIV), which contains four times as much antigen as the standard dose, offers superior protection against hospitalization and death.

medical.title: Do you lose your whole day to one appointment? 'Waiting mode' may be why
medical.summary: You have a 3 p.m. appointment. It's now 10 a.m., and somehow your entire day already feels out of reach. Maybe you find yourself unable to start anything properly. You feel on edge, waiting for something to begin or end. You check the time again and again. Even a positive, planned event, like a friend visiting later, can leave you feeling stuck.

medical.title: COPA mutations reveal alternative trigger for small intestine tumors
medical.summary: A signaling system known as the Wnt pathway plays a central role in how cells in the intestine grow, divide and renew themselves. Decades of research have shown that disruption of this pathway is a defining feature of many intestinal cancers. In particular, mutations in the APC gene—which normally acts as a brake on Wnt signaling—are widely recognized as a key initiating event in colorectal tumors.

medical.title: The high cost of undiagnosed and untreated ADHD: Unequal mental‑health access and the care economy
medical.summary: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a common neurodevelopmental disorder that can persist from childhood into older adulthood. It affects individuals from all ethnic groups and socioeconomic backgrounds.

medical.title: Parkinson's medication shows promise in treating treatment-resistant depression
medical.summary: For many people who suffer from depression, the condition is not just about feeling down but also about a loss of motivation and difficulty finding pleasure in activities they used to enjoy. A new study conducted in Sweden shows that a medicine used to treat Parkinson's disease can be used as an add-on therapy to alleviate these symptoms in some patients with treatment-resistant depression. The study has been published in Nature Medicine.

medical.title: Kids should be involved in their health care. Here's how to make that happen
medical.summary: Children have a right to learn, play and grow. To help children thrive, parents and health care professionals must ensure they get the medical support they need. However, existing evidence shows we could involve children more in their health care appointments.

medical.title: Adding T3 to standard thyroid treatment may ease lingering symptoms in hypothyroidism
medical.summary: A working professional who describes herself as "a tired mom of two" believes she's gaining the upper hand on a condition that's made her struggle with fatigue, weight gain, lethargy and hair loss.

medical.title: Cerebellar nets may regulate social behavior and help explain autism-linked circuit changes
medical.summary: Researchers at Kanazawa University have identified a previously unrecognized mechanism by which structural changes in the cerebellum influence social behavior. The study demonstrates that disruption of specialized extracellular structures surrounding cerebellar neurons alters neuronal activity across brain circuits involved in social behavior. The findings provide new insight into the neural mechanisms associated with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). The work is published in the journal Translational Psychiatry.

medical.title: Why drinking alcohol makes you reach for chips and nachos
medical.summary: Have you ever wondered why savory foods like chips, nachos and salted nuts go so well with a beer or glass of wine? And why sometimes you feel an insatiable appetite for junk food while drinking?

medical.title: A common newborn procedure faces new scrutiny as evidence undercuts one widely blamed cause of breastfeeding trouble
medical.summary: A joint study by the University of Oulu and Oulu University Hospital suggests that a newborn's upper lip frenulum is unlikely to be a major cause of breastfeeding difficulties. The study, published in JAMA Network Open, followed 264 mother–infant pairs at Oulu University Hospital between 2023 and 2024. Researchers assessed the anatomy and mobility of the upper lip frenulum in healthy, full-term infants and compared the findings with mothers' reported breastfeeding experiences.

medical.title: These tiny holes could change how the world cleans water
medical.summary: A new nature-inspired membrane uses perfectly uniform one-nanometer pores to filter molecules with remarkable precision. The technology could transform industries such as pharmaceuticals and textiles by reducing energy consumption, improving water reuse, and delivering separation performance far beyond current filters.

medical.title: Can fasting fight gum disease? Scientists find surprising link
medical.summary: A low-calorie fasting-style diet significantly reduced inflammation linked to gum disease in a small clinical study. The findings suggest that what people eat may influence gum health almost as much as what they do with a toothbrush.

medical.title: Scientists discover a surprising cancer link to Alzheimer’s disease
medical.summary: Researchers discovered that mutations linked to blood cancers may help trigger Alzheimer’s disease by creating overly inflammatory immune cells in the brain. The unexpected finding could lead to new blood-based screening methods and potential treatments borrowed from cancer medicine.

medical.title: Stanford scientists regrow lost cartilage and reverse arthritis in major breakthrough
medical.summary: A new treatment that blocks an aging-related protein restored lost cartilage in old mice and helped prevent arthritis after knee injuries. Human cartilage samples showed similar signs of regeneration, raising hopes for a future drug that could repair joints instead of replacing them.

medical.title: Your brain starts making social decisions before you do
medical.summary: Researchers found that social behavior begins in the brain before it becomes visible as movement. In zebrafish, a coordinated pattern of activity spread across the brain several seconds before the animals approached another fish. A higher brain region called the pallium played a key role, and fish with stronger neural signals were generally more social.

medical.title: One fat helped pancreatic cancer grow while another cut disease in half
medical.summary: A surprising new study suggests that when it comes to pancreatic cancer, the kind of fat you eat may matter more than how much. Researchers found that oleic acid—the main fat in olive oil and several other common foods—sped up tumor growth in mice predisposed to pancreatic cancer, while omega-3-rich fats from fish oil dramatically slowed disease development.

medical.title: Researchers block key protein that helps Parkinson’s spread through the brain
medical.summary: A newly identified protein called GPNMB may play a major role in helping Parkinson’s disease spread through the brain. Researchers discovered that immune cells release the protein in response to damaged neurons, creating a vicious cycle that speeds up brain cell degeneration. In early experiments, antibodies that blocked GPNMB stopped the toxic process from spreading between cells.

science.title: Dragonfly and damselfly migrations crisscross planet, with 100 species confirmed
science.summary: Migration flights of dragonflies and damselflies crisscross much of our planet, new research reveals. Scientists from the universities of Exeter and Lund reviewed global evidence and found 100 dragonfly and damselfly species that migrate, and a further 85 possible migratory species. This means that only 1.5% of all dragonfly and damselfly species migrate (or 2.9% including possible migrants)—but the migrations can include millions of individuals, and some travel thousands of kilometers. The study is published in Biological Reviews.

science.title: AI tracks missing hydrogen atoms in crystals with 97% success rate
science.summary: Artificial intelligence is often used to generate images. In research, specialized AI models are used for scientific applications—for example, to predict the positions of atoms in materials. The MatterGen model developed by Microsoft can generate complex crystal structures from just a few pieces of information—which atoms should be present and in what proportions—and researchers can then use these structures for computer simulations of new materials.

science.title: A cornerstone of Milky Way history may need rewriting with evidence of multiple ancient mergers
science.summary: Astronomers may have uncovered new details about one of the Milky Way's most important ancient collisions. Using data from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) and a new clustering algorithm, researchers have found evidence suggesting the famous Gaia-Sausage/Enceladus structure (GSE) has a far more complicated origin than previously thought.

science.title: New findings complete first evolutionary history of all living millipede orders, dating back 460 million years
science.summary: Long before vertebrates walked on land, millipedes had the place to themselves. Hundreds of millions of years before dinosaurs arrived, these early decomposers were helping establish Earth's terrestrial ecosystems. But despite their ancient history, scientists still hadn't fully unraveled their evolutionary story.

science.title: Photo: Hubble and Webb offer new view of Black Eye Galaxy
science.summary: This March 20, 2026, image of Messier 64, or the Black Eye Galaxy, is a composite view from NASA's Hubble Space Telescope and James Webb Space Telescope. It shows Messier 64 captured at near- and mid-infrared wavelengths by Webb, while Hubble's image shows the galaxy in ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared light.

science.title: SpaceX launches Falcon 9 rocket minutes ahead of IPO
science.summary: SpaceX launched a Falcon 9 rocket loaded with Starlink satellites Friday less than an hour before Elon Musk's company was set to lift off for what would be the largest IPO in Wall Street history.

science.title: SpaceX lifts off in record Wall Street debut
science.summary: Elon Musk's SpaceX began its first day as a public company on Wall Street on Friday after the biggest initial public offering in history, with the polarizing entrepreneur promising he will take humanity to Mars.

science.title: Would you buy milk from a gene‑edited cow? Consumers may be more open than you think
science.summary: As temperatures rise, New Zealand's dairy farmers face a growing challenge: keeping cows cool enough to remain productive. Heat stress can reduce milk production, harm animals and lower the environmental efficiency of dairy farming. For an economy so heavily reliant on dairy exports, the stakes are significant.

science.title: Why birds ignore Newton: New theory could sharpen models of flocks, crowds and cells
science.summary: Birds in flocks, bacteria and cells: In many collective systems, individual elements respond to only part of their surroundings, seemingly defying Newton's third law of motion—action equals reaction. These exceptions are known as nonreciprocal interactions. A Dresden physics team working with Roderich Moessner, a founding member of the Würzburg–Dresden Cluster of Excellence ctd.qmat, has now developed a theory that makes it possible to describe these interactions efficiently and simulate them far more precisely.

science.title: Venus flytrap's snap may come from rapid cell wall softening, not water flow
science.summary: The Venus flytrap (Dionaea muscipula) is a marvel of nature, a highly effective killer that doesn't have to move an inch to capture and kill its prey. It releases a fruity nectar scent to attract flies and other insects. After they land in the trap, tiny hairs are triggered and the leaves shut with impressive speed.

science.title: Aliens might exist, but there are three reasons why they're not visiting us
science.summary: The United States government's recent release of hundreds of previously classified unidentified anomalous phenomena (UAPs) cases spanning the 1940s to the present, along with the new Steven Spielberg movie, "Disclosure Day," about extraterrestrial life, has fueled the idea that aliens are visiting Earth.

science.title: Why chickens come in so many colors, and what one gene reveals about evolution
science.summary: From snow white and jet black to golden brown, domestic chickens display a wider range of plumage colors than almost any other livestock species. A new international study, with researchers from Leipzig University playing a key role, explains why: A single gene is capable of producing this full spectrum. The study provides an example of how genetic diversity and visible traits can emerge within a short evolutionary period. The findings have now been published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

science.title: Black Eye Galaxy
science.summary: This March 20, 2026, image of Messier 64, or the Black Eye Galaxy, is a composite view from NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope and James Webb Space Telescope. It shows Messier 64 captured at near- and mid-infrared wavelengths by Webb, while Hubble’s image shows the galaxy in ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared light. Messier 64 is characterized […]

science.title: DNA tracking links raccoons to riverborne bacteria with possible human spillover
science.summary: The emerging infectious bacterium Escherichia albertii has caused outbreaks of severe food poisoning and hospitalized people through contaminated water and foods, such as salad ingredients. Now, a new study from Osaka Metropolitan University (OMU) has gathered evidence from river, animal and genetic samples that suggests a pathway by which invasive raccoons (Procyon lotor) transmit infections to humans.

science.title: What Amazonian photographs reveal when we look again, and together
science.summary: A new paper by Junia Mortimer, Urban Studies Foundation fellow, explores what happens when photographic archives are revisited through different conceptual frameworks, and with different people in the room. The archive in question belongs to Roberto Monte-Mór, professor at the Federal University of Minas Gerais, architect, economist and urban planner, who worked in and photographed Amazonian frontier settlements throughout the 1970s, 1980s and 1990s while developing his concept of extended urbanization.

science.title: Swapping steak for salmon could boost health and reduce emissions
science.summary: Swapping a beef steak for salmon once a week could save the amount of carbon needed to fly from London Heathrow to Morocco, according to new research by the universities of Bristol and Southampton. The study, published in Environmental Research: Food Systems, found the U.K.'s current level of meat consumption is 2 to 3 times higher than recommended guidelines.

science.title: Slime molds make decisions using internal fluid flows
science.summary: Despite lacking brains or nervous systems, slime molds are capable of making surprisingly sophisticated decisions: navigating mazes, finding food and even remembering where they found it last time. How they manage to do all this without any neural architecture has long puzzled researchers.

science.title: Can ancient bacteria help solve one of agriculture's biggest challenges?
science.summary: During the Archean Eon—roughly 4 billion years ago—the Earth was a lifeless planet. The atmosphere lacked oxygen, and there were few, if any, organisms to be found anywhere on the globe. Then something incredible happened. Microscopic bacteria appeared in freshwater lakes, likely in what is now present-day Australia. These bacteria—known now as cyanobacteria—had the near-magical ability to convert sunlight and water into oxygen and other chemical energy.

science.title: Ancient DNA study of post-Roman Europeans reveals emergence of complex new society
science.summary: A new study from the HistoGenes project, of which Patrick Geary, professor emeritus in the School of Historical Studies, is co-PI, is helping scholars frame a better picture of the early medieval people who inhabited Western Europe after the fall of the Roman Empire, as well as the societies they created.

science.title: Giant underground neutrino detector brings scientists closer to cracking the neutrino puzzle
science.summary: Deep beneath the ground in China, the massive JUNO neutrino observatory has delivered its first major scientific breakthrough, achieving one of the most precise measurements yet of how elusive neutrinos change as they travel. Using just 59 days of data, researchers sharply improved measurements of key neutrino properties, boosting confidence that JUNO can tackle one of particle physics' biggest mysteries: determining the true mass hierarchy of neutrinos.

science.title: The missing notebooks that solved a 55-million-year-old fossil mystery
science.summary: A spectacular fossil fish discovered on a remote cliff in New Zealand nearly 30 years ago has finally revealed its full story thanks to an unexpected discovery: the original collector’s long-lost field notebooks. The 1.2-meter fossil, preserved in stunning three-dimensional detail, belonged to an ancient tarpon-like predator that cruised New Zealand waters about 55 million years ago.

science.title: A legendary golden fabric lost for 2,000 years has returned
science.summary: Researchers in South Korea have recreated the legendary “sea silk” once prized by emperors, using fibers from a clam cultivated in Korean coastal waters. They discovered that its famous golden shine comes from tiny protein structures that reflect light rather than from pigments or dyes. Because the color is built into the fiber’s structure, it can remain vibrant for centuries.

science.title: Ancient DNA shared with Neanderthals may explain human language
science.summary: A tiny set of ancient genetic “switches” may have played a surprisingly large role in making human language possible. Researchers found that these DNA regions, which act like volume controls for genes involved in brain development, have an outsized influence on language ability despite making up less than 0.1% of the genome.

environment.title: Experts say ‘bare bones’ US laws are unfit to regulate nascent deep-sea mining industry
environment.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

environment.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
environment.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

environment.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
environment.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

environment.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
environment.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

environment.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
environment.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

environment.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
environment.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

environment.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
environment.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

environment.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

mix_items.title: 😂 Cine se aseamănă se adună! Prunea a găsit la piaţă o roşie cu moţul de 40 cm
mix_items.summary: Cine se aseamănă se adună, spune o vorbă din bătrâni! Aflat la piaţă pentru cumpărăturile de zi cu zi, fostul fotbalist Florin Prunea a găsit pe tarabă o roşie cu moţ complet neobişnuită. mix_items.title: Se dă startul la proiectarea tronsoanelor 1 și 3 ale Autostrăzii Moţca - Iaşi – Ungheni. Când s-ar putea intra în faza de execuție

Absolventă a Drexel University din Philadelphia și cu experiență profesională acumulată atât în SUA, cât și în România, ea s-a specializat în dezvoltarea brandurilor din zona de wellness și personal development. Parcursul său, de …

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... mix_items.title: Dragonfly and damselfly migrations crisscross planet, with 100 species confirmed
mix_items.summary: Migration flights of dragonflies and damselflies crisscross much of our planet, new research reveals. Scientists from the universities of Exeter and Lund reviewed global evidence and found 100 dragonfly and damselfly species that migrate, and a further 85 possible migratory species. This means that only 1.5% of all dragonfly and damselfly species migrate (or 2.9% including possible migrants)—but the migrations can include millions of individuals, and some travel thousands of kilometers. The study is published in Biological Reviews.

mix_items.title: Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor
mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: A higher-dose flu shot could spare millions of older adults a hospital stay
mix_items.summary: Influenza is a seasonal condition that causes coughing, sneezing, mild fever and aches in most cases. However, it can sometimes take a serious turn, leading to hospitalization, especially for young children, adults over 65 and pregnant people. A recent study published in JAMA Network Open examined whether the high-dose inactivated influenza vaccine (HD-IIV), which contains four times as much antigen as the standard dose, offers superior protection against hospitalization and death.

mix_items.title: Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor
mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: AI tracks missing hydrogen atoms in crystals with 97% success rate
mix_items.summary: Artificial intelligence is often used to generate images. In research, specialized AI models are used for scientific applications—for example, to predict the positions of atoms in materials. The MatterGen model developed by Microsoft can generate complex crystal structures from just a few pieces of information—which atoms should be present and in what proportions—and researchers can then use these structures for computer simulations of new materials.

mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... mix_items.title: A cornerstone of Milky Way history may need rewriting with evidence of multiple ancient mergers
mix_items.summary: Astronomers may have uncovered new details about one of the Milky Way's most important ancient collisions. Using data from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) and a new clustering algorithm, researchers have found evidence suggesting the famous Gaia-Sausage/Enceladus structure (GSE) has a far more complicated origin than previously thought.

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... mix_items.title: Do you lose your whole day to one appointment? 'Waiting mode' may be why
mix_items.summary: You have a 3 p.m. appointment. It's now 10 a.m., and somehow your entire day already feels out of reach. Maybe you find yourself unable to start anything properly. You feel on edge, waiting for something to begin or end. You check the time again and again. Even a positive, planned event, like a friend visiting later, can leave you feeling stuck.

mix_items.title: New findings complete first evolutionary history of all living millipede orders, dating back 460 million years
mix_items.summary: Long before vertebrates walked on land, millipedes had the place to themselves. Hundreds of millions of years before dinosaurs arrived, these early decomposers were helping establish Earth's terrestrial ecosystems. But despite their ancient history, scientists still hadn't fully unraveled their evolutionary story.

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Photo: Hubble and Webb offer new view of Black Eye Galaxy
mix_items.summary: This March 20, 2026, image of Messier 64, or the Black Eye Galaxy, is a composite view from NASA's Hubble Space Telescope and James Webb Space Telescope. It shows Messier 64 captured at near- and mid-infrared wavelengths by Webb, while Hubble's image shows the galaxy in ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared light.

mix_items.title: COPA mutations reveal alternative trigger for small intestine tumors
mix_items.summary: A signaling system known as the Wnt pathway plays a central role in how cells in the intestine grow, divide and renew themselves. Decades of research have shown that disruption of this pathway is a defining feature of many intestinal cancers. In particular, mutations in the APC gene—which normally acts as a brake on Wnt signaling—are widely recognized as a key initiating event in colorectal tumors.

mix_items.title: SpaceX launches Falcon 9 rocket minutes ahead of IPO
mix_items.summary: SpaceX launched a Falcon 9 rocket loaded with Starlink satellites Friday less than an hour before Elon Musk's company was set to lift off for what would be the largest IPO in Wall Street history.

mix_items.title: The high cost of undiagnosed and untreated ADHD: Unequal mental‑health access and the care economy
mix_items.summary: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a common neurodevelopmental disorder that can persist from childhood into older adulthood. It affects individuals from all ethnic groups and socioeconomic backgrounds.

mix_items.title: SpaceX lifts off in record Wall Street debut
mix_items.summary: Elon Musk's SpaceX began its first day as a public company on Wall Street on Friday after the biggest initial public offering in history, with the polarizing entrepreneur promising he will take humanity to Mars.

mix_items.title: Would you buy milk from a gene‑edited cow? Consumers may be more open than you think
mix_items.summary: As temperatures rise, New Zealand's dairy farmers face a growing challenge: keeping cows cool enough to remain productive. Heat stress can reduce milk production, harm animals and lower the environmental efficiency of dairy farming. For an economy so heavily reliant on dairy exports, the stakes are significant.

mix_items.title: Why birds ignore Newton: New theory could sharpen models of flocks, crowds and cells
mix_items.summary: Birds in flocks, bacteria and cells: In many collective systems, individual elements respond to only part of their surroundings, seemingly defying Newton's third law of motion—action equals reaction. These exceptions are known as nonreciprocal interactions. A Dresden physics team working with Roderich Moessner, a founding member of the Würzburg–Dresden Cluster of Excellence ctd.qmat, has now developed a theory that makes it possible to describe these interactions efficiently and simulate them far more precisely.

mix_items.title: Parkinson's medication shows promise in treating treatment-resistant depression
mix_items.summary: For many people who suffer from depression, the condition is not just about feeling down but also about a loss of motivation and difficulty finding pleasure in activities they used to enjoy. A new study conducted in Sweden shows that a medicine used to treat Parkinson's disease can be used as an add-on therapy to alleviate these symptoms in some patients with treatment-resistant depression. The study has been published in Nature Medicine.

mix_items.title: Kids should be involved in their health care. Here's how to make that happen
mix_items.summary: Children have a right to learn, play and grow. To help children thrive, parents and health care professionals must ensure they get the medical support they need. However, existing evidence shows we could involve children more in their health care appointments.

mix_items.title: Venus flytrap's snap may come from rapid cell wall softening, not water flow
mix_items.summary: The Venus flytrap (Dionaea muscipula) is a marvel of nature, a highly effective killer that doesn't have to move an inch to capture and kill its prey. It releases a fruity nectar scent to attract flies and other insects. After they land in the trap, tiny hairs are triggered and the leaves shut with impressive speed.

mix_items.title: Aliens might exist, but there are three reasons why they're not visiting us
mix_items.summary: The United States government's recent release of hundreds of previously classified unidentified anomalous phenomena (UAPs) cases spanning the 1940s to the present, along with the new Steven Spielberg movie, "Disclosure Day," about extraterrestrial life, has fueled the idea that aliens are visiting Earth.

mix_items.title: Adding T3 to standard thyroid treatment may ease lingering symptoms in hypothyroidism
mix_items.summary: A working professional who describes herself as "a tired mom of two" believes she's gaining the upper hand on a condition that's made her struggle with fatigue, weight gain, lethargy and hair loss.

mix_items.title: Cerebellar nets may regulate social behavior and help explain autism-linked circuit changes
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Kanazawa University have identified a previously unrecognized mechanism by which structural changes in the cerebellum influence social behavior. The study demonstrates that disruption of specialized extracellular structures surrounding cerebellar neurons alters neuronal activity across brain circuits involved in social behavior. The findings provide new insight into the neural mechanisms associated with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). The work is published in the journal Translational Psychiatry.

mix_items.title: Why chickens come in so many colors, and what one gene reveals about evolution
mix_items.summary: From snow white and jet black to golden brown, domestic chickens display a wider range of plumage colors than almost any other livestock species. A new international study, with researchers from Leipzig University playing a key role, explains why: A single gene is capable of producing this full spectrum. The study provides an example of how genetic diversity and visible traits can emerge within a short evolutionary period. The findings have now been published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

mix_items.title: Black Eye Galaxy
mix_items.summary: This March 20, 2026, image of Messier 64, or the Black Eye Galaxy, is a composite view from NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope and James Webb Space Telescope. It shows Messier 64 captured at near- and mid-infrared wavelengths by Webb, while Hubble’s image shows the galaxy in ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared light. Messier 64 is characterized […]

mix_items.title: Why drinking alcohol makes you reach for chips and nachos
mix_items.summary: Have you ever wondered why savory foods like chips, nachos and salted nuts go so well with a beer or glass of wine? And why sometimes you feel an insatiable appetite for junk food while drinking?

mix_items.title: DNA tracking links raccoons to riverborne bacteria with possible human spillover
mix_items.summary: The emerging infectious bacterium Escherichia albertii has caused outbreaks of severe food poisoning and hospitalized people through contaminated water and foods, such as salad ingredients. Now, a new study from Osaka Metropolitan University (OMU) has gathered evidence from river, animal and genetic samples that suggests a pathway by which invasive raccoons (Procyon lotor) transmit infections to humans.

mix_items.title: What Amazonian photographs reveal when we look again, and together
mix_items.summary: A new paper by Junia Mortimer, Urban Studies Foundation fellow, explores what happens when photographic archives are revisited through different conceptual frameworks, and with different people in the room. The archive in question belongs to Roberto Monte-Mór, professor at the Federal University of Minas Gerais, architect, economist and urban planner, who worked in and photographed Amazonian frontier settlements throughout the 1970s, 1980s and 1990s while developing his concept of extended urbanization.

mix_items.title: Swapping steak for salmon could boost health and reduce emissions
mix_items.summary: Swapping a beef steak for salmon once a week could save the amount of carbon needed to fly from London Heathrow to Morocco, according to new research by the universities of Bristol and Southampton. The study, published in Environmental Research: Food Systems, found the U.K.'s current level of meat consumption is 2 to 3 times higher than recommended guidelines.

mix_items.title: Slime molds make decisions using internal fluid flows
mix_items.summary: Despite lacking brains or nervous systems, slime molds are capable of making surprisingly sophisticated decisions: navigating mazes, finding food and even remembering where they found it last time. How they manage to do all this without any neural architecture has long puzzled researchers.

mix_items.title: A common newborn procedure faces new scrutiny as evidence undercuts one widely blamed cause of breastfeeding trouble
mix_items.summary: A joint study by the University of Oulu and Oulu University Hospital suggests that a newborn's upper lip frenulum is unlikely to be a major cause of breastfeeding difficulties. The study, published in JAMA Network Open, followed 264 mother–infant pairs at Oulu University Hospital between 2023 and 2024. Researchers assessed the anatomy and mobility of the upper lip frenulum in healthy, full-term infants and compared the findings with mothers' reported breastfeeding experiences.

mix_items.title: These tiny holes could change how the world cleans water
mix_items.summary: A new nature-inspired membrane uses perfectly uniform one-nanometer pores to filter molecules with remarkable precision. The technology could transform industries such as pharmaceuticals and textiles by reducing energy consumption, improving water reuse, and delivering separation performance far beyond current filters.

mix_items.title: Can fasting fight gum disease? Scientists find surprising link
mix_items.summary: A low-calorie fasting-style diet significantly reduced inflammation linked to gum disease in a small clinical study. The findings suggest that what people eat may influence gum health almost as much as what they do with a toothbrush.

mix_items.title: Can ancient bacteria help solve one of agriculture's biggest challenges?
mix_items.summary: During the Archean Eon—roughly 4 billion years ago—the Earth was a lifeless planet. The atmosphere lacked oxygen, and there were few, if any, organisms to be found anywhere on the globe. Then something incredible happened. Microscopic bacteria appeared in freshwater lakes, likely in what is now present-day Australia. These bacteria—known now as cyanobacteria—had the near-magical ability to convert sunlight and water into oxygen and other chemical energy.

mix_items.title: Ancient DNA study of post-Roman Europeans reveals emergence of complex new society
mix_items.summary: A new study from the HistoGenes project, of which Patrick Geary, professor emeritus in the School of Historical Studies, is co-PI, is helping scholars frame a better picture of the early medieval people who inhabited Western Europe after the fall of the Roman Empire, as well as the societies they created.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a surprising cancer link to Alzheimer’s disease
mix_items.summary: Researchers discovered that mutations linked to blood cancers may help trigger Alzheimer’s disease by creating overly inflammatory immune cells in the brain. The unexpected finding could lead to new blood-based screening methods and potential treatments borrowed from cancer medicine.

mix_items.title: Stanford scientists regrow lost cartilage and reverse arthritis in major breakthrough
mix_items.summary: A new treatment that blocks an aging-related protein restored lost cartilage in old mice and helped prevent arthritis after knee injuries. Human cartilage samples showed similar signs of regeneration, raising hopes for a future drug that could repair joints instead of replacing them.

mix_items.title: Giant underground neutrino detector brings scientists closer to cracking the neutrino puzzle
mix_items.summary: Deep beneath the ground in China, the massive JUNO neutrino observatory has delivered its first major scientific breakthrough, achieving one of the most precise measurements yet of how elusive neutrinos change as they travel. Using just 59 days of data, researchers sharply improved measurements of key neutrino properties, boosting confidence that JUNO can tackle one of particle physics' biggest mysteries: determining the true mass hierarchy of neutrinos.

mix_items.title: The missing notebooks that solved a 55-million-year-old fossil mystery
mix_items.summary: A spectacular fossil fish discovered on a remote cliff in New Zealand nearly 30 years ago has finally revealed its full story thanks to an unexpected discovery: the original collector’s long-lost field notebooks. The 1.2-meter fossil, preserved in stunning three-dimensional detail, belonged to an ancient tarpon-like predator that cruised New Zealand waters about 55 million years ago.

mix_items.title: A legendary golden fabric lost for 2,000 years has returned
mix_items.summary: Researchers in South Korea have recreated the legendary “sea silk” once prized by emperors, using fibers from a clam cultivated in Korean coastal waters. They discovered that its famous golden shine comes from tiny protein structures that reflect light rather than from pigments or dyes. Because the color is built into the fiber’s structure, it can remain vibrant for centuries.

mix_items.title: Ancient DNA shared with Neanderthals may explain human language
mix_items.summary: A tiny set of ancient genetic “switches” may have played a surprisingly large role in making human language possible. Researchers found that these DNA regions, which act like volume controls for genes involved in brain development, have an outsized influence on language ability despite making up less than 0.1% of the genome.

mix_items.title: Experts say ‘bare bones’ US laws are unfit to regulate nascent deep-sea mining industry
mix_items.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

mix_items.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
mix_items.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

mix_items.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
mix_items.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

mix_items.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
mix_items.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

mix_items.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
mix_items.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

mix_items.title: Your brain starts making social decisions before you do
mix_items.summary: Researchers found that social behavior begins in the brain before it becomes visible as movement. In zebrafish, a coordinated pattern of activity spread across the brain several seconds before the animals approached another fish. A higher brain region called the pallium played a key role, and fish with stronger neural signals were generally more social.

mix_items.title: One fat helped pancreatic cancer grow while another cut disease in half
mix_items.summary: A surprising new study suggests that when it comes to pancreatic cancer, the kind of fat you eat may matter more than how much. Researchers found that oleic acid—the main fat in olive oil and several other common foods—sped up tumor growth in mice predisposed to pancreatic cancer, while omega-3-rich fats from fish oil dramatically slowed disease development.

mix_items.title: Researchers block key protein that helps Parkinson’s spread through the brain
mix_items.summary: A newly identified protein called GPNMB may play a major role in helping Parkinson’s disease spread through the brain. Researchers discovered that immune cells release the protein in response to damaged neurons, creating a vicious cycle that speeds up brain cell degeneration. In early experiments, antibodies that blocked GPNMB stopped the toxic process from spreading between cells.

mix_items.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
mix_items.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

mix_items.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
mix_items.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am pus viata in ordine. Acum nu mai gasesc nimic.

satire_ro.title: Gigi Becali și-a comandat un sarcofag din aur pentru locul de veci din Pipera
